title: Angular Lifecycle Hooks – Managing Component Life

meta.title: Angular Lifecycle Hooks Guide – ngOnInit, ngOnChanges & ngOnDestroy (2026)
meta.description: Master the lifecycle of Angular components. Learn when and how to use ngOnInit, ngOnChanges, and ngOnDestroy to build high-performance, leak-free applications.

content.value: An Angular component has a lifecycle managed by the framework. From the moment it is instantiated to the moment it is destroyed, Angular provides 'hooks'—specific methods you can implement to tap into key moments. As a Technical Lead, mastering these hooks is essential for managing heavy resources like the Revochamp AI engine, ensuring you initialize data correctly and clean up memory to prevent leaks.
While there are many hooks, a few are used in almost every professional component. You implement these by adding the corresponding interface to your class and defining the method.

value: **ngOnChanges()**: Responds when Angular sets or resets data-bound input properties (@Input). It receives a SimpleChanges object.
**ngOnInit()**: Called once after the first ngOnChanges. This is the best place to fetch data from your Python APIs.
**ngOnDestroy()**: Called just before Angular destroys the component. Use this to unsubscribe from Observables and stop timers.

content.value: Sometimes you need to interact with the DOM or child components. Hooks like `ngAfterViewInit` are triggered only after Angular has fully initialized the component's views and child views.

faq.question: Why use ngOnInit instead of the Constructor?
faq.answer: The constructor is a TypeScript feature for class instantiation, but Angular hasn't finished setting up the component (like @Input properties) yet. ngOnInit is an Angular-specific hook that ensures all inputs and DI services are ready for use.

faq.question: When should I use ngOnDestroy?
faq.answer: Every time you use a manual '.subscribe()' in your TypeScript, you should unsubscribe in ngOnDestroy. This prevents memory leaks, especially in single-page applications where components are frequently created and removed.

faq.question: What is the SimpleChanges object?
faq.answer: In ngOnChanges, Angular passes a SimpleChanges object that contains the previous and current value of every changed @Input property. This allows you to compare values and trigger logic only when specific inputs change.

quiz.question: Which lifecycle hook is called first when a component is initialized with inputs?
Where is the best place to perform a cleanup, such as unsubscribing from an Observable?
True or False: ngOnInit is called every time a component's input changes.
